10:08 PM
The Moth and the Lamp We pity the moth for confusing a lamp for the moon. Yet here you are confusing a screen for the world. And that one stings a little if you're honest, because we laugh at the moth—this tiny creature drawn to artificial light thinking it found something real, burning itself chasing something that was never the source. But scroll back through your last hour. How much of what you consumed was real? How much of what you felt was genuine versus performed for you by an algorithm that knows exactly what keeps you looking? The screen isn't the world. It's a lamp. A very bright, very convincing, very addictive lamp. And the world—the actual world—is still out there with real light, and real texture, and real conversations that don't disappear after 24 hours. The mountain doesn't care how many people saw you climb it. The sunset doesn't perform better for the camera. The people who actually love you are not behind that glass. The moth can't help itself, but you can.
13Roz 7/5/2026 10:08 PM
Bug
10:09 PM

The Moth and the Lamp We pity the moth for confusing a lamp for the moon. Yet here you are confusing a screen for the world. And that one stings a little if you're honest, because we laugh at the moth—this tiny creature drawn to artificial light thinking it found something real, burning itself chasing something that was never the source. But scroll back through your last hour. How much of what you consumed was real? How much of what you felt was genuine versus performed for you by an algorithm that knows exactly what keeps you looking? The screen isn't the world. It's a lamp. A very bright, very convincing, very addictive lamp. And the world—the actual world—is still out there with real light, and real texture, and real conversations that don't disappear after 24 hours. The mountain doesn't care how many people saw you climb it. The sunset doesn't perform better for the camera. The people who actually love you are not behind that glass. The moth can't help itself, but you can.

10:38 PM
Forwarded
Just because you love the ocean doesn't mean you have to drown in it. We often confuse love with sacrifice. We think the deeper we feel something, the more we have to give up for it, as if the more we suffer, the more proof there is that we truly care. So we stay in things that are pulling us under, and we call it loyalty—the relationship that constantly drains your energy, the friendship that takes more than it gives, or the version of yourself you keep shrinking to fit someone else's narrative. But there's a difference between something that moves you and something that takes you. And you wonder why you feel more exhausted the deeper you swim, and it still never feels like enough, because you don't have to fully submerge yourself in order for it to change you
Originally sent: 7/5/2026 10:36 PM
